speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number seven. The survivor is Mania Lichtenstein. Today is April 1, 1996. Who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, we liberated-- I and the group I was wandering with-- ourselves. Because we were going towards the Russian-- joined the Russian-- occupied Russian-- the cities that the Russians occupied. recaptured, actually, from the Germans, the Polish cities. And we started to-- well, we weren't very warmly greeted. Because the Russians were very bitter. It was a very bad war for them too. And they felt that you know, the Jews, maybe, weren't doing their part in the war. They were-- they were trying to get out of it. Anyway, this-- and became like, in a way, anti-Semitic. They did not appreciate us, which is-- which was a great disappointment to us. Because we felt that after what went through, everybody would want to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, we survived like, the-- the impossible. How on earth did we make it? We thought they'll be interested.

speaker: interviewee
text: But no, they were not. And as a matter of fact, they would not-- because during the Russian occupation, you had to register in order to even sleep over, one night, in a place. It was very, very strict. Very suspicious and strict. It was war. They were afraid of spies or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they would not let us register in that certain city. So we had to move on, just like gypsies. Move on. Move on. Move on, city to city to city. Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we ended up going to a Rovno, which is another big city in our part there, in that province, I would say. It was called [POLISH]-- no, yeah. [POLISH]. So we went to that big city. Again, they would not let us register.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we stayed on. Where else should we go? I mean, they don't [? want that-- ?] we have no place to go. Where did you get food to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Water to drink?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't-- you know, we-- we-- there were Jewish houses empty. So we went into a house. If we found something-- and you were able at that time, because you were already like, a free-- it was-- it was war, a terrible war. But a free-- you were ad you were a free person among free people. War is one thing. But you are still free. You can walk out of the house. You can walk. You can stand on your head. You can do anything you want. You're just like anybody else, like the Poles, or anyone else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that sense of freedom feel to you--

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it felt--

speaker: interviewer
text: After what you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: It felt-- oh, it felt very mighty and very-- but it was still very blemished. Because night after night, bomb raids. There were bombs. The fire was-- the-- the sky was like one ball of fire. And we could hear. We would, ooh, we'd lie down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every time you'd hear a plane approach, we run into bunkers. But just like the Poles, you know? Because we were free-- free people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody's out to kill us. The bomb might, but that's already another story. I'm-- I'm still-- now I'm a person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have dreams at that point and goals and thoughts of the future--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. Not yet, no. Oh yeah, I want to be, one day, one day, when the war will end, we'll go into a bed, undress. Not in your clothes. Because we always slept in them. Even the-- you took off your shoes, they were sitting just that you are ready to slip in. This was the greatest dream, to undress, go into a bed, and sleep. That was a-- that was a dream not easily to be materialized.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, how? When will it ever end? How will you sleep in a bed? Whose bed are you going to sleep? [LAUGHS] You had this dream often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the-- the-- yeah. This, oh, to undress. You just wanted to take your clothes off and lie down. And maybe even sleep and forget about the whole world. But as soon as it gotten dark, every night, bombs. And we ran into the-- the cellar. And that-- you know, bunker. And we see fire all over. But as it was destined, it didn't hit us. One night, I was with two more. Yeah, now I remember another name that was in that little shed. You asked me-- oh no, the little shed-- yeah, no. Sorry, sorry. One day I was-- like I remembered another name.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can feel free to mention.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. But this wasn't-- the one I was in that-- when the-- when the bomb fell-- but had-- yeah, she was in the little shed. But not the six girls that I lived with in-- in the second ghetto. The bomb fell on our building, removed all the upper floors. And the bottom floor stays. Of course, we-- so I and that friend of mine, Paper and Leah Boksenbojm, she was no relative to ours, but Boksenbojm. And she died last year. And we were all covered with debris. But we screamed. We called for help. When the raid was over they dug us out. And we were alive again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the run again? Were you still--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, yes. We didn't stay long in one place because we were not allowed to. And I don't know. And also, we wanted to go back home. Home was-- hardly, you can call it home. There was no Jewish house left. There's no Jew left. But it's the next thing, you know, that you can call home, to your hometown. So we then started moving-- as the-- as the Russians used to progress more and more to the west, they recaptured city after city. It-- It was going like magic, then. And you know, hopes were so high that eventually it will-- there-- there will be an end to it. Because it seemed like this-- this war will never end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have thoughts, already, that you were going to live and survive this war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, no place for thoughts or for dreams or-- no. Just existing, existing, and making the day. Just actually-- yeah, this is what I'll say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Living today. Tomorrow? Who knows? Who cares? Who cares? Who knows, tomorrow? Living day by day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just that, oh yes. As a matter of fact, we still had that [POLISH], you should call it, or that concept, that tomorrow, tomorrow, what's tomorrow? Today. Only today. Even when were already in the free world. But we still-- maybe we didn't know any better. We were young. And we had no means.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whatever we had. Today I was rich if I had $1 on me. Tomorrow, who knows?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so we move on with the Russian army. We-- we followed the-- the Russians. We'd you know, go right next-- follow them, until they liberate Wlodzimierz. When we heard, already-- at that time, we could hear, already, you know, the Russians were there. There were radios. You could-- you-- you-- you knew what was happening in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have to go back. Because where else do you go? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we eventually made it back to our hometown. What did you hope to find in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. But-- but-- but where do you go? Where is it better? Who wants you? You-- when you go, you have to have a-- a [? zeal-- ?] help-- help me out, maybe. You have to have a-- a purpose or a-- how do you say it?

speaker: interviewer
text: A goal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, you know, a goal. Yeah. I'm-- I'm-- I'm heading towards something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? Who wants me? Who needs me? Where do I belong? No relatives, nothing. So it's home. You didn't-- did you expect to find any relatives? Or hoping?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you were always hoping. Oh, this year, if-- if you ask me about hopes, because as the war subdued, or right after the war, you would hear about people finding, suddenly, a brother. This was something. Oh, maybe. I knew that they were all gone. Because while being in-- I didn't tell you many, many things. While being in the ghettos, I sneaked out to my-- to my house, which wasn't in the ghetto. It was dead, already, there. Nothing left. Because I wanted to find some pictures that I-- this was my-- which I did. And there was-- like, the-- the Germans, when they came, they destroyed everything. I don't know. Maybe they were looking for some valuables.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as I say, we had-- my father had like, big boxes of powder. Not like to-- for like, wholesale probably. Maybe they were selling it then in grams, you know? And there was this much powder on the floor, all over the house. Because they thought-- maybe they thought maybe they'll find something. What do they need the powder for? So I went on my knees. And I dug.

speaker: interviewee
text: Surely enough, I found so many pictures of my family. Carried them in my pocket day and night. I should have this as-- was my most important things that I wanted to salvage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and-- but then, until my pockets tore in my coat. And I had to put the pictures out. And when the pogroms suddenly took place, I-- I ran without them. So-- So where were we? So we come to our hometown-- With hopes of maybe finding somebody, but not too realistic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh, not really, no. No, no, no, no. Later on, well, just who knows? Maybe somebody will-- you know, when they were having these rumors in Canada-- I don't know if you were aware. There was these Nazi that came up, these Nazi-- neo-Nazis. And that it's a hoax, and it's not true. And they just fabricated lies.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would say, oh, God, help me. Let it be true, that it's all fabricated, it's a lie, it's a bad dream. But then I would say, so where is my family? If this didn't take place, where are they? Or anybody or my whole-- I-- never mind my family, my whole neighborhood, my whole class. Where is another kid, survived? You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's class that pictures-- the picture I showed you, there's one girl who-- I-- I don't even know. I didn't hear. But they said that she survived. Somewhere in Israel, she lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so I hope they are right, that it was only a big lie and that nothing happened. So where are they, if nothing happened? So you went back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we came to-- to our hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Found destruction. Jewish homes were leveled. Of course, there were still some empty houses, even maybe, Poles. I don't know. We-- we kind of settled somewhere in a-- in a-- in a structure. Like, the few-- there were another few people that we met there. Because people came back to their place of origin. That was the most known to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and then we started getting such reaction from the Poles, and screaming at us. How dare you? We thought you were already-- they thought that we were an extinct species. How do we show up again? You know, like, stunned. How-- how dare you come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were afraid. We were just running. So we-- we left Poland. Not Poland, but we left our hometown. Also, the Russians were there. It was really bad. The Russians would arrest you in the middle of the street, and you wouldn't even find out why.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's say somebody would say that you stole or you-- you did something, whatever. They'll just come, grab you by the arm, take you to the-- to jail.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you never knew why. You never-- never had any-- anything to say about it. You were doomed.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were so afraid. So we started running too, from the Russians. Well, but if you-- you couldn't escape. Because this was, already, the better of the two evils. You know? So where did you go to next?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we left the city. I say we, because, again, the group that we met, we always left in groups. It was an extended family. And we started going from city to city. We wanted to go see who else survived, you know finding Jews. So we went to Hrubieszow. And that was all in-- in our province, I would call it, in our state. In Polish, it was [POLISH]. You know, it's-- this is the area. We went to Hrubieszow. Then we went to Lublin. Lublin was already-- that was-- we were there in May. We were there a few months, actually. And May 8, 1945, when-- when they announced that the war was over, this is, I remember very well. You know, your head swelled.

speaker: interviewer
text: War over. Well-- well, this was another dream. You know? About surviving, that remained to be seen. You know, you were still running. You had no time to stop and think, do I want to live? What do I want to do? You run.

speaker: interviewee
text: But if the war ends, the feeling, alive without war. This, yeah, this was another dream that you thought about a lot. Oh, after the war. And sure enough, proclamation--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you first heard--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the war is over. May, I remember. Somebody started to argue with me, it wasn't the date. But I remember. I don't know it from books. I know it from real life that May 8, 1945, the war was proclaimed over.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's when you first heard it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you start singing? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not yet. No, not anymore. We just sang when we were still together. And no, didn't sing. No, we didn't sing. Later on-- later on, in my older life, I sing a lot. I like to sing. It's kind of an outlet o all your emotions. It's like, more than talking, confiding in somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go after you heard the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed on as long as possible. There was-- was already a black market. You can-- if you find-- then when we came back to our hometown, my husband, they knew some peasants who had-- that the parents left a few gold coins.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, a Jew was always like, leaving something for-- who knows what could happen? For a rainy day. Some-- who knows? Something out of the ordinary. So when they were put in the ghetto, before they were put-- they left these with that peasant that in case they survive, they would have this. So my husband and my brother-in-law, his brother-- He wasn't your husband yet, right? He was your-- no.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When I-- oh, I forgot to mention that when I came back to Lubna, to Wlodzimierz-- and since we were really together already, we cared for one another, we decided to get married. And that was another story. But I'm not-- oh, that's a funny story.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, married. I didn't even know-- if somebody ask me, who is giving you away? What? What's this? I didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- but anyway, it was just a few survivors that were there. And somebody baked a cake. And-- and there was vodka. And the Russians who were stationed in my hometown, they heard that there is a wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: What more do you want? You know? There is vodka. They stormed in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, they gave them all the vodka. That was the only drink that-- that we knew then. I don't know. And-- and they just-- and they wanted the bride. [LAUGHS] No-- no-- no one else but the bride. All evening, I was locked up in a room, and they were banging on the door. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They had to lock me up. They wanted the bride. You know? It was funny.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, we-- so we got married. So then we started-- when we were running to Hrubieszow, Lublin, and Lublin--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were already man and wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- and then the war was over. Of course, times were very morbid, because you had nothing. And-- and it was chaos, like, chaos after a miserable long war.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we also heard that in that big city of Lodz, there is an organized Jewish, like, committee, I would call it. Somebody-- you know, we needed somebody to take care, to tell us where do you go from here to here? Excuse me. I am recovering from a cold. I must explain. So we are heading to Lodz. When we come there, sure enough, there was a committee set up, an office, kind of. To-- and all the other survivors were drifting in there. They-- they knew that this is where we're going to-- you know, you need to be organized.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you don't know what your next step is going to be. And life was getting more and more livable. You can go in the market. There was black market still. You know, it wasn't all--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband had these gold coins still?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But-- but first, when we were in that place where I was bombed, where we had bombed, the whole group of people ate because he exchanged them. And-- and we bought food. And the whole group was feeding because there wasn't such a thing as mine and-- we-- it's-- it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was sharing among--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish-- I wish it would be like this, in this world of ours, that you go a little bit beyond just I and me and me and I. And there wasn't such a thing like, oh, it's mine, and I-- I'll eat and you will starve. No. We ate. We all-- it was equally served.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know, people started coming out and on the black market, even selling a secondhand dress or a pair of shoes. Because how long can this one dress last? And the one shoe-- one pair of shoes? And-- where am I?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I talk too much. So you-- you've found this committee that was going to help?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they-- and they-- OK, and then you say OK, now we have somebody to take care of us, to tell us what's happening in the world and what is going to be with us. And they told us about in Berlin, there is an aliyah going from Greece, I think it was. Ships, going from Berlin, we go to Greece. And from there, go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because this was our next destination. You have to go home, somewhere that you can call your home. And Palestine was, of course, in dream of-- in dreams of Jews for ages, you know? [NON-ENGLISH], you know? Jerusalem was-- was your place where you have to end, you know? And-- Were you-- were you looking forward to going there? Is that some place you wanted to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who care-- no. Who cares? This is where they let us go. I mean, we did not-- we did not choose because the climate is nice, or I can make a nice living, or I don't know what is nice. No, no. They-- we had no-- we still had no feelings and no demands and no dreams. We just have to go where they let us in. We want to go somewhere where I'm-- where I'm let in, where they open the door and say, OK, come in. And we go, Palestine, this is the place. Europe is blood-soaked. We do not want to stay in Europe. We-- we do not want to. We refuse to stay in Europe. Because this is we step on blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is not-- and what if our children grow up? You know, at that time, you are still very-- who are they going to marry? I mean, you were supposed to be Jewish because they made you think that even if you do not want to or you try to forget, no way can you. So we have to go to Palestine. This is our-- our land. You know? And we go to Berlin. We go in cattle trains, jumping off, and off trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, my husband, he was very-- I would never survive these times. It's-- I survived all this only because of faith. I ran after people. I survived here. I ran after this. I must-- I'm sorry. I wish I can give myself some credit, but I can't. But my husband, my brother-in-law, you know, two young, healthy boys. And we jump off the trains. You know, because you couldn't even buy a ticket and go somewhere. Impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even Germany was still-- it's impossible. Poland, Germany, we had to run from Poland to Germany, to Berlin. And we come there. And we get connected with that Jewish UNRRA, maybe, it was called?

speaker: interviewee
text: UNRRA. United Nations Refugees-- U, something. It's for the refugees, set up to guide them, what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, this was a secret, an illegal aliyah to go to Israel. You couldn't go buy a ticket and just make a voyage. You know? And we sit and wait. And of course, they-- they-- they have our names, when our turn will come. But as soon as they send the ship out and they reach the shores of Israel, the English would not let them in. Were you on this ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Not yet. They wouldn't let them in. You know this. You know the story. I always say, if somebody doesn't know, if you saw the movie, if you read the book, by Leon Uris, when they came with the boat. But they-- they weren't let into Palestine, the English. So they was-- ended up sitting in Cyprus, and then had to return back. So at that point, we knew that the dream is all blown over, no more. Because we cannot go to Palestine. In the meantime, we are very young. Life, to hell they go. I don't know if I should use this expression. The Germans, they know how to live, that the whole world should learn from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's not like living, more or less, whatever. Life is so-- immediately operas are open. The operettas are open, a movie theater. You know, it's-- it's-- I know it's difficult, like, for people to say, how could you go? Everything that happened was-- was eradicated. You know? Like, it's-- I don't know how the mind works. It did not happen. Nothing happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you're young. Maybe if-- when you were old, you cannot say, no. But you're young. You have your youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And suddenly, it's like a rosebud. You know? It's nothing. There is only a stem, and suddenly it's full blown. Because you're young.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess this is the secret of love-- like-- excuse me. Excuse me. [LAUGHS] Oh, boy, I'm making a fool of myself. This is the secret of life, maybe, that, hey, suddenly there is a beautiful bloom. We started going into the movies. We didn't even know or understand the language then. But live. And the sun was shining. It was so warm. It was just like something-- after a horrible, horrible nightmare, suddenly, hey, it's beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then again, you would like to understand, how could you stay on the German soil? For the first time, we were-- because the Germans did not know the difference. They not like the Pole.

speaker: interviewee
text: He would just have to smell and know that you're Jewish. The German didn't know unless you told him. So we would walk the street. And nobody would look at me. And if I speak, and we spoke German. Before you knew, I was reading German books. And I really was good at it. And-- and-- and when we spoke, we didn't have to like, all-- all cringed, all-- all crumpled. Because, like, in Polish, Shh. Don't talk. Don't do it. You know, afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because nobody knew who you are. And if I spoke and they sensed, they detected an accent, they thought I'm Greek, I'm Italian, I'm anything but Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Berlin? We-- we stayed with-- we stayed nine months, actually, in Berlin. And then there was a refugee camp in-- in Berlin. And so we-- and there were a lot of Jews there. So we-- because like in the-- in the-- it was in-- in Berlin, near Berlin. So we went to that refugee camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I always remembered. Oh my god. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forgot. Unless I can-- I can't put it in later. Oh, I-- this, I just forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went there, stayed a-- not too long. From there, we went to-- they thought-- I don't know, wherever they thought-- first of all, we were-- we liked to roam around. Because we were going from camp to-- especially my husband. He was a very caring, family person, kind of. Although he was young, but looking for a relatives. My brother-in-law, he was younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where are you going? You know, go look. Go find a-- a pin in an ocean. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was going for-- so we were going from camp to camp. Then we went-- we heard there is another camp, Camp [NON-ENGLISH] in Eschwege Hessen, was the name of the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to that camp. And there were quite a few Jews, you know, from all over Poland, or wherever, not even Poland, in that camp. And life was already-- it was, of course, very primitive. We lived in a-- like, in a barrack. We-- we didn't-- we have-- the floors were cement. You know? Not-- Really lived-- no, I can't even call it primitive. But just enough to survive. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, they were barracks for the armies. The [GERMAN] were stationed there. They were like military barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given food to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- you know, this is another thing. I-- I'm trying to remember one meal. I don't remember. Meantime, I-- like, I got conceived and-- when I was in Berlin. And all this running and jumping off trains, pregnant, and didn't know any better. Jumping off moving trains. [LAUGHS] I-- when you're young. And you just have to, also. You have to. And in Eschwege, I gave birth to my little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Jeane. Her name was [? Gote, ?] but then, oy, she might not like it. She said she didn't like the name because it kind of German sounded. or-- so she changed it to Jeane. And then, other friends came from another camp. That's another story. Maybe I should eliminate it. It's actually not friends. I-- I didn't know these people very well, only through somebody who lived in Russia. And he worked in our hometown. It's just too much--

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you decide to go from Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they came, and they said-- no, Berlin, we went to Eschwege.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. From Eschwege.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the camp I remember now-- Schlachtensee. Schlachtensee, the-- the-- from Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the first camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Displaced persons? Displaced persons camp? Is that what it  was called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, oh yes, only. Only, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, displaced persons.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, because from then, everybody was crawling out from the holes. And hey, I'm here. I'm alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what will you do with me? So you had to guide people what to do next, where to go.
